text.title: AI Feature Eval Set Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the ai-feature-eval-set-design methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Eval set MUST contain ≥30 gold rows, ≥10 adversarial rows, ≥5 drift-watch rows. Total ≥ 45 rows; below that, the eval cannot detect regressions reliably.
rule.rationale: Statistical power calculation on a binary judge with α=0.05 and detectable effect 10pt requires ≥40 trials; round up + add adversarial.

rule.statement: When the task output is open-ended (free text, summary, chat reply), the judge MUST be LLM-as-judge with a structured Pydantic rubric (`evidence`, `meets_criteria`, `failure_modes`). Free-form judges ('rate 1-5') are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Free-form judges have inter-rater noise > 30%; structured rubrics drop it to < 10% (Eugene Yan design-an-eval).

rule.statement: Eval set MUST be versioned (`eval_set_id`, semver). Each PR that adds/removes rows bumps the version. CI compares results within a version; cross-version comparisons require an explicit re-baseline step.
rule.rationale: Comparing scores across eval-set versions without re-baselining is the most common false-positive in eval ops.

rule.statement: Drift-watch rows MUST be sampled from the most recent 30 days of production traffic (anonymized). They are refreshed monthly.
rule.rationale: Production distribution drifts; static eval misses real users' new patterns.

rule.statement: CI MUST fail a PR whose eval score drops > 2pp on any axis (gold accuracy, adversarial robustness, drift accuracy). Waivers require explicit reason in PR body.
rule.rationale: 2pp is below typical inter-run noise; smaller deltas are reported but not blocking.
